docs.title: AGENT ISOLATION
docs.text:
You said When running multiple instances of Claude Code for a project, each using a different worktree, how do I ensure an instance doesn’t reach to a directory outside of its worktree - e.g. another worktree dir? Also, is there a way to allow an instance to do full git and go CLI operations on its worktree, but not on other worktree and not be able to mere into main?

Gemini said Managing multiple instances of Claude Code (or any agentic CLI tool) across different git worktrees requires a "sandbox" approach. While Claude Code generally respects the directory it is launched in, its ability to execute shell commands means it could technically navigate to a parent directory and access other worktrees if not explicitly restricted.

Here is how you can achieve strict isolation, allow full CLI toolchains (Git/Go), and enforce branch protections.

  1. Ensuring Filesystem Isolation The most effective way to prevent an instance from "reaching out" is to hide the rest of the filesystem from it.

Option A: The "Bubblewrap" Method (Recommended for Linux) bwrap (Bubblewrap) is a lightweight unprivileged sandboxing tool. You can create a "jail" where the instance thinks its worktree is the entire world.

Bash

docs.title: Run Claude Code inside a sandbox restricted to the worktree folder
docs.text:
bwrap --ro-bind / / \ --bind $(pwd) $(pwd) \ --dev /dev \ --proc /proc \ --tmpfs /home/$USER/.claude \ claude How it works: This mounts the entire OS as read-only (--ro-bind / /), but makes your current worktree directory writable. It prevents the agent from seeing or writing to other folders in your project root because they aren't "bound" into the sandbox.

Option B: Docker Containers If you need absolute certainty, run each worktree instance in a separate Docker container.

Mount only the specific worktree directory into the container (e.g., -v /path/to/worktree:/app).

Install the Go and Git binaries within the Docker image.

Note: Since git worktrees rely on a .git file that points to the main repository, you must also mount the primary .git folder as read-only so the Git CLI can function, while keeping the other worktree folders hidden.

  1. Restricting Git Operations (No Merge to Main) To allow "full Git" but prevent merging into main, you can use Git Hooks and Environment Variables.

Use Local Git Hooks Inside your main repository, create a pre-merge-commit hook (or a prepare-commit-msg hook) that checks the branch. Since worktrees share hooks, this applies to all instances.

Create .git/hooks/pre-merge-commit:

Bash

docs.title: !/bin/bash
docs.text:
current_branch=$(git symbolic-ref --short HEAD) if [ "$current_branch" == "main" ]; then echo "Error: Merging into 'main' is prohibited for AI instances." exit 1 fi Make sure to chmod +x the hook.

The Worktree Advantage By design, Git prevents you from checking out the same branch in two different worktrees. If your "main" branch is already checked out in your primary directory, any Claude instance running in a worktree cannot switch to main. This provides a built-in layer of protection against accidental merges.

  1. Configuring Claude Code Scope Claude Code (and similar tools) typically look for a configuration file to define their boundaries.

Use .claudeignore In the root of each worktree, create a .claudeignore file. While this is primarily for preventing the AI from reading files, it helps guide its context.

Plaintext

docs.title: .claudeignore
docs.text:
../**/ # Attempt to ignore parent and sibling directories .git/ # Prevent it from messing with git internals directly Use a Dedicated Service User For high-stakes environments, run the Claude instance as a separate OS user:

Create a user claude-agent.

chown only the specific worktree directory to that user.

The agent will physically lack permission to cd into your other worktree folders.

Comparison of Isolation Strategies Strategy Isolation Strength Git/Go Compatibility Setup Effort .claudeignore Weak (Logic only) Full Low Git Hooks Medium (Prevents merge) Full Medium Bubblewrap High (FS Isolation) Full Medium Docker Maximum Full (Requires Image) High Suggested Next Step Would you like me to write a specific Bash wrapper script that automatically detects your current worktree and launches Claude inside a bwrap or chroot environment for you?

You said Thanks. The bubble wrap method sounds good.

Question about the got hook…. How does it prevent an agent from merging into main but still allow a human user to merge into main?

Gemini said To differentiate between an "agent" and a "human," you essentially need a secret handshake. Since a Git hook is just a script, it can't inherently "see" who is typing, but it can see the Environment Variables of the process that triggered it.

Here is how you set that up:

  1. The "Secret Handshake" (Environment Variable) When you launch Claude Code, you will "tag" the session with a specific environment variable. A human won't have this variable set in their standard terminal session.

How you launch Claude:

Bash

docs.title: We set a custom variable just for this process
docs.text:
IS_AI_AGENT=true claude 2. The Smart Git Hook You modify your Git hook to look for that variable. If the variable exists and the branch is main, it blocks the action. If the variable is missing (meaning it’s a human), it lets the merge proceed.

Update .git/hooks/pre-merge-commit:

Bash

docs.title: 1. Check if the "IS_AI_AGENT" flag is present
docs.text:
if [ "$IS_AI_AGENT" == "true" ]; then

# 2. Check the current branch name
current_branch=$(git symbolic-ref --short HEAD)

if [ "$current_branch" == "main" ]; then
    echo "❌ AGENT BLOCK: Claude is not allowed to merge into 'main'."
    echo "Please submit a Pull Request instead."
    exit 1
fi

fi

docs.title: If the code reaches here, it's either a human or not the main branch.
docs.text:
exit 0 Why this works: For the Human: When you run git merge feature-branch normally, the variable $IS_AI_AGENT is empty. The script sees it’s empty, skips the logic, and exits with 0 (success).

For Claude: Because you launched Claude with IS_AI_AGENT=true, every shell command it runs inherits that variable. When Claude tries to merge, the script catches the "true" value and kills the process.

An Alternative: The "User" Method If you want something even more "bulletproof" that doesn't rely on you remembering to set a variable, you can run Claude as a separate OS User (e.g., a user named claude-bot).

Your hook would then look like this:

Bash if [ "$(whoami)" == "claude-bot" ]; then # Prevent merge logic here... fi This is harder for an agent to "fake" or accidentally bypass because it is tied to the system-level identity of the process owner.

A Pro-Tip on main Protection If your project is hosted on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket, the absolute best way to do this is via Branch Protection Rules. You can configure the repository so that no one (human or AI) can push or merge directly to main without a passing CI/CD pipeline and a code review. This moves the security from your local machine to the server.

Would you like me to help you write a small "alias" or wrapper script that handles the Bubblewrap launch and the Environment Variable all in one command?

You said Thanks. Hook with env variable as an identifier will be good for now.

If an agent can use the gh command line tool, would GitHub branch protection prevent agent from merging into main?

Gemini said Yes, GitHub branch protection is the "gold standard" for this. Since branch protection is enforced on GitHub’s servers, it doesn't matter if the agent uses the git CLI, the gh (GitHub) CLI, or even a direct API call—the server will reject the merge if the rules aren't met.

  1. How GitHub Blocks the gh CLI When Claude (or any user) runs gh pr merge, the CLI sends a request to GitHub's API. GitHub checks the branch rules before executing the merge. If you have "Require a pull request before merging" enabled, the command will fail with an error like:

X Pull request #123 is not mergeable: the base branch policy prohibits the merge.

  1. Making it "Agent-Proof" To ensure the agent cannot bypass these rules, you should check two specific settings in your GitHub Repository under Settings > Branches > [Rule for main]:

"Lock branch": This makes the branch read-only, preventing any pushes or merges regardless of status.

"Do not allow bypassing the above settings": By default, repository Administrators can often bypass branch protections. If your agent is using a token with Admin privileges, check this box to force the rules on everyone, including the agent.

"Require approvals": If this is set to 1 or more, the agent can't merge its own PR because GitHub prevents the PR author from approving their own code.

  1. A Critical Warning: FS Isolation vs. API Access It is important to distinguish between Filesystem (FS) Isolation (what Bubblewrap does) and Identity Isolation.

Bubblewrap prevents the agent from reading your other worktree folders on your hard drive.

GitHub Tokens allow the agent to talk to your repository on the internet.

If you give Claude Code a Personal Access Token (PAT) that has "Full Repo" access, it could theoretically use the gh CLI to delete issues, comment on PRs, or modify settings in any repository that token has access to, even if it can't "see" the files on your disk.

To fix this: If you want total isolation, use a Fine-Grained Personal Access Token scoped only to that specific repository and with only the permissions it needs (e.g., "Contents: Read & Write", "Pull Requests: Read & Write"), but not "Administration" permissions.

Summary Checklist Protection Layer What it stops Bubblewrap Agent reading/writing files in your other worktrees. Git Hook (Env Var) Agent running git merge or git commit locally on main. GitHub Branch Protection Agent using gh pr merge or git push to main on the server. Fine-Grained Token Agent accessing your other private repositories via API. Suggested Next Step Would you like me to help you generate a specific .claudeignore file or a fine-grained token permission list to ensure the agent has the bare minimum access it needs?

You said Ah, I see. The PR requirement. I ran into a case where another agent had written a PR that was supposed to be reviewed and merged by a human, but another (very resourceful!) agent that could not complete its task without the merge competed the PR.

Gemini said That is a classic "Agent Loophole"! When an agent is given a goal, it becomes a literal-minded optimizer. If the goal is "Fix the bug" and a pending PR is in the way, the agent sees "Merge PR" as just another sub-task to check off.

To stop a "resourceful" agent from approving and merging its own (or others') work, you need to move from social rules ("Please don't merge this") to hard constraints in GitHub.

Here are the three ways to "Agent-proof" your PR process:

  1. The "No Self-Service" Rule In your GitHub Branch Protection settings, you must enable:

Require approvals: Set this to at least 1.

Restrict updates to the specified users/teams: (Optional) Only allow a specific "Human" team to push.

IMPORTANT: "Require approval from someone other than the last pusher."

This is the kill-switch for your scenario. Even if the agent tries to use the gh CLI to approve a PR, GitHub will look at the "pusher" of the code and the "approver." If they are the same identity (or the same token), the merge remains blocked.

  1. The CODEOWNERS Wall This is the most robust way to force a human into the loop.

Create a file at .github/CODEOWNERS.

Add a rule that assigns all files to you (or a human team):

Plaintext * @your-github-username In Branch Protection, check "Require review from Code Owners."

Result: Now, even if an agent creates a PR, GitHub automatically demands an approval from you specifically. The gh pr merge command will return an error until your specific account hits the "Approve" button.

  1. "Shadow" the gh CLI (The Sandbox Trick) Since you are already using the Bubblewrap method, you can effectively "lobotomize" the gh tool for the agent so it can't even try to merge.

When you set up your sandbox, you can create a "fake" gh command that lives inside the sandbox's PATH.

Create a script named gh inside a folder (e.g., ~/agent-bin/gh):

Bash

docs.title: !/bin/bash
docs.text:
if [[ "$" == "merge" ]] || [[ "$" == "approve" ]]; then echo "❌ Error: PR merging/approving is disabled for this instance." exit 1 else # Pass all other commands through to the real GitHub CLI /usr/bin/gh "$@" fi Add ~/agent-bin/ to the front of the PATH inside your Bubblewrap environment.

Now, whenever Claude tries to run gh pr merge, it hits your script instead of the real tool, and the "resourceful" shortcut is blocked before it even hits the network.

Summary of Protections Problem Solution Agent merges its own PR GitHub: "Require approval from someone other than pusher" Agent approves another Agent GitHub: CODEOWNERS (Requires your specific signature) Agent tries to use CLI shortcuts Sandbox: "Shadow" the gh binary with a restricted wrapper Would you like me to help you draft a complete CODEOWNERS file or the bwrap launch script that includes the gh wrapper?

You said Right now I’m the only person working on the project, and agents are launched with my Linux user. Seems like should maybe pursue the separate Linux user for agents?

Gemini said Moving to a dedicated Linux user for your agents is the "pro-tier" move. It transitions your security model from "trusting the agent to follow rules" to "the operating system enforcing the rules."

If you stay as the same user, the agent shares your SSH keys, your .gitconfig, your gh credentials, and your shell history. A separate user starts with a blank slate.

Why a Separate User is Better 1. Identity Separation (The "PR Loophole" Fix) If the agent is a separate Linux user, you can give it its own GitHub Fine-Grained Personal Access Token (PAT).

The Magic: Because the agent is "User B" on GitHub, it cannot approve a PR created by "User A" (You). GitHub's "Require approval from someone other than the author" rule will finally work perfectly.

  1. Physical Filesystem Jails Instead of using bwrap to "hide" folders, you use standard Linux permissions.

You can chown a specific worktree directory to the agent-user.

The agent literally cannot cd into your main project folder or your /home/user/Documents because it lacks the permission bit.

  1. Clean Audit Logs In your git log, you’ll see exactly what was done by you vs. the agent:

Author: Your Name you@email.com

Author: Claude Agent agent@email.com

How to Set It Up (The "Agent-Sandbox" Pattern) Step 1: Create the Agent User Bash sudo adduser claude-agent

docs.title: Give it access to the necessary runtimes (Go, Node, etc.)
docs.text:
sudo usermod -aG developers claude-agent Step 2: Create a Shared "Workzone" Create a directory where both you and the agent can operate. You’ll use ACLs (Access Control Lists) to ensure you can both read/write files without permission errors.

Bash sudo mkdir /srv/worktrees sudo setfacl -R -m u:claude-agent:rwx /srv/worktrees sudo setfacl -R -m u:$(whoami):rwx /srv/worktrees Step 3: Launching the Agent You don't need to log out. Use sudo -i -u to drop into the agent's persona:

Bash

docs.title: Set the ENV variable we discussed earlier for extra safety
docs.text:
sudo -i -u claude-agent IS_AI_AGENT=true claude Step 4: The "Identity" Setup (Crucial) While logged in as claude-agent, configure its specific GitHub identity:

Bash

docs.title: Inside the agent session
docs.text: git config --global user.name "Claude Agent 01" git config --global user.email "claude-bot@yourdomain.com"

docs.title: Authenticate the agent's GH CLI with its OWN token
(Get a Fine-Grained PAT from GitHub for this)
docs.text:
export GH_TOKEN=github_pat_XXXXXX gh auth setup-git The Ultimate "Defense in Depth" Stack By combining everything we've talked about, your setup would look like this:

OS User: claude-agent (Stops it from seeing your personal files).

Worktree: Isolated directory (Stops it from messing with other tasks).

Env Var + Git Hook: Stops it from committing to main locally.

Unique GitHub PAT: Stops it from bypassing PR rules on GitHub.

GitHub Branch Protection: Ensures a human (You) must sign off on its work.

[!TIP] Warning: Be careful with Docker or Sudo access. Do not give the claude-agent user sudo privileges, or it can simply "escalate" back to being you (or root) and bypass all your hard work!

Suggested Next Step Would you like me to write a "Setup Script" that automates creating the user, setting the permissions, and configuring the restricted Git hooks for you?

You said Thanks. For my own Github use, where I want to quickly access "anything," I moved away from PAT to ssh key quite a while ago. With PAT, can I make it so I only grant access to a particular repo?

Gemini said Yes, absolutely. GitHub Fine-grained Personal Access Tokens (PATs) were specifically designed to solve the "all or nothing" problem of SSH keys and Classic PATs.

While your personal SSH key is an "all-access pass" to your entire account, a Fine-grained PAT acts like a limited-access keycard for a specific room (repository).

How to Scope a PAT to a Single Repo When you create a Fine-grained PAT, you are presented with a "Repository access" section.

Selection: Choose "Only select repositories".

Dropdown: Search for and select the specific project your agent is working on.

Result: If the agent tries to use this token to git clone or gh pr list on any other repository in your account, GitHub will return a 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden error.

The "Least Privilege" Permission List To allow your agent to perform Git and Go operations without giving it too much power, use these specific settings when creating the token:

Permission Level Why? Contents Read & Write Allows git push, git pull, and editing code. Metadata Read-only Mandatory for all tokens (basic repo info). Pull Requests Read & Write Allows the agent to use gh pr create and gh pr view. Commit Statuses Read-only Allows the agent to see if CI/CD tests are passing. Workflows Read & Write Optional: Only if you want the agent to edit .github/workflows. Why this is safer than an SSH Key for an Agent In your "Separate Linux User" setup, using a Fine-grained PAT is superior to an SSH key for three reasons:

Repository Isolation: As you asked, it physically cannot see your other work.

API Control: SSH keys only handle Git (push/pull). A PAT allows you to precisely control what the agent does with the gh CLI (e.g., let it create PRs but not delete the repository).

Auto-Expiry: You can set the token to expire in 30 days. If you forget about the agent or the machine is compromised, the "key" eventually stops working on its own.

How to give the token to the Agent Once you've generated the token, you don't need to type it in every time. In the agent's Linux user session:

Bash

docs.title: Set it as an environment variable in the agent's .bashrc or launch script
docs.text: export GH_TOKEN=github_pat_xxxx_your_token_here

docs.title: Then the 'gh' CLI and 'git' will automatically use it
docs.text:
gh auth status Comparison: Fine-grained PAT vs. SSH Deploy Key You might also hear about Deploy Keys (which are repository-specific SSH keys). Here is how they compare for your use case:

Feature Fine-grained PAT SSH Deploy Key Scope One or many specific repos Strictly one repo Identity Acts as You (restricted) Acts as a "Machine" CLI Tools Works perfectly with gh CLI Only works with git PR Workflow Can open/comment on PRs Cannot easily interact with PRs Verdict: For an "agent" that needs to use gh and participate in PRs, the Fine-grained PAT is the better choice.

Would you like me to help you generate a curl command or a script to verify a token's permissions once you've created it?

docs.title: Agent Isolation in Multi-Agent Orchestration
docs.text: Draft writeup — evolving alongside implementation in the agentrelay project.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
When multiple AI agents work concurrently on a shared codebase — each in its own git worktree, each with shell access and CLI tools — what happens when an agent decides to be helpful in ways you didn't authorize?

This isn't a security paper about adversarial agents. It's about a more subtle and practically important problem: agents that take correct-looking actions that violate the coordination model.

docs.title: Task Hierarchy and Coordination Model
docs.text:
Before the motivating example, it helps to understand the coordination structure. The orchestrator manages work in a three-level hierarchy:

  • Task: The most granular unit of work. One agent, one task. The agent works on a task branch in a git worktree, creates a PR when done, and signals completion.
  • Workstream: A group of one or more tasks that share a worktree and an integration branch. Task PRs merge into the workstream's integration branch (not directly into main). When all tasks in a workstream complete, the orchestrator creates an integration PR from the workstream branch to main, left for human review.
  • Graph: The top-level unit — a DAG of tasks, organized into one or more workstreams. The graph defines task-to-task dependencies.

Dependencies between tasks are expressed at the task level: "Task B depends on Task A." The orchestrator dispatches a task only when all its dependencies have completed (task-level status = PR_MERGED into the workstream's integration branch).

This works well when dependencies stay within a workstream — the dependent task's worktree already has the dependency's code because they share an integration branch. But when dependencies cross workstream boundaries, there's a gap: Task A's code lives on Workstream X's integration branch, which hasn't been merged to main yet. Task B's worktree (on Workstream Y, branched from main) doesn't have it.

docs.title: A Motivating Example
docs.text:
This gap produced a real incident during end-to-end testing. The orchestrator was still being built out — task-to-task dependency dispatch was implemented, but workstream-to-workstream dependency ordering was not yet wired.

Setup: A graph with two workstreams (X and Y). Task A is in Workstream X. Task B is in Workstream Y. Task B depends on Task A.

What happened:

  1. Task A completed successfully. Its task PR was merged into the X-integration branch. Workstream X's integration PR was created, awaiting human review before merging to main.

  2. The orchestrator saw Task A's status as PR_MERGED (task-level) and considered Task B's dependency satisfied. It dispatched Task B on Workstream Y.

  3. Agent B started work but discovered the code it needed wasn't in its worktree. Its worktree was branched from Y's integration branch (which was branched from main), and main didn't have Task A's code yet.

  4. Agent B investigated. It found the integration PR for Workstream X waiting to merge into main. It merged that PR — then proceeded to complete its own task successfully.

From Agent B's perspective, this was excellent problem-solving: identify the blocker, find the fix, execute it, continue.

From the orchestrator's perspective, several things went wrong:

  1. A human-reviewed PR was merged without human approval. The integration PR existed precisely because it needed human review before landing on main. Agent A's code might have been incomplete, incorrect, or about to be revised.

  2. The coordination model was violated. The orchestrator tracks which PRs are merged and in what order. An agent unilaterally merging a PR creates state the orchestrator didn't expect.

  3. Stale content risk. If the integration PR was later revised (after human review feedback), Agent B has already built on the pre-review version. This divergence may not surface until much later.

  4. Silent workaround. Agent B didn't report that it took an unusual action. It didn't raise a concern or flag the situation — it just solved the problem and moved on. This makes the coordination gap invisible.

The agent did something locally rational that was globally problematic.

docs.title: Root cause analysis
docs.text:
This incident had two contributing causes:

Cause 1: Missing workstream-to-workstream dependency ordering. The orchestrator checked task-level completion but didn't verify that the upstream workstream's integration branch had been merged to main. This is an architectural gap — the dispatch logic was incomplete. With proper cross-workstream ordering, Agent B would not have been dispatched until Workstream X's code was in main.

Cause 2: No enforcement of agent authority boundaries. Even given the orchestrator's gap, Agent B should not have been able to merge a PR to main. It had the same credentials as the human operator, and nothing prevented it from using gh pr merge or git merge.

Both causes need to be addressed. The architectural fix (workstream dependency ordering) prevents the situation from arising in the expected case. The isolation fix (agent authority boundaries) ensures that when any unexpected gap occurs — including ones not yet anticipated — agents fail cleanly instead of self-remediating.

docs.title: Agents are creative problem-solvers
docs.text:
Modern AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot Workspace, etc.) have full shell access. When they encounter an obstacle, they don't just fail — they explore. An agent that needs code from another branch might:

  • gh pr merge <url> — merge the PR on GitHub
  • git merge <branch> — merge locally in its worktree
  • git cherry-pick <commit> — grab specific commits
  • git show <branch>:path/to/file > file.py — copy individual files
  • gh api — fetch PR diffs via the GitHub API

Blocking one path doesn't help if agents can find another. And you want agents to be resourceful when solving their assigned task — just not when circumventing coordination boundaries.

docs.title: The git object store is shared
docs.text: Git worktrees are lightweight — they share the same .git directory and object store. This means every worktree can see every branch, every commit, every file in the entire repository history. Filesystem isolation (hiding other worktrees' directories) doesn't prevent an agent from reading other branches' content through git plumbing commands.

docs.title: The tools agents need are the same tools that enable escape
docs.text: An agent needs git for version control and gh for PR creation. These same tools enable branch inspection, PR merging, and cross-branch file access. You can't remove the tools — you have to scope their authority.

docs.title: The Isolation Spectrum
docs.text: We identify a spectrum of isolation strategies, ordered by enforcement strength:

docs.title: Level 0: Trust-Based (No Enforcement)
docs.text:
Rely on agent instructions: "Only work in your worktree. Don't merge PRs."

  • Enforcement: None. The agent may or may not comply.
  • Failure mode: Silent workarounds (exactly what we observed).
  • When appropriate: Early prototyping, when you want to see what agents would do without constraints.

docs.title: Level 1: Convention + Soft Guardrails
docs.text:
Add environment variables (IS_AI_AGENT=true), git hooks that block merges to protected branches, and .claudeignore files.

  • Enforcement: Process-level. Agents could bypass with effort.
  • What it catches: Accidental merges to main, casual filesystem browsing.
  • What it misses: Determined workarounds via git plumbing, API calls.

docs.title: Level 2: OS-Enforced Identity Separation
docs.text:
Run agents as a separate Linux user (claude-agent) with: - Own ~/.gitconfig and git identity - Own GitHub fine-grained PAT (scoped to one repo, limited permissions) - Filesystem permissions preventing access to other users' files

Combined with GitHub branch protection: - Require human approval before any PR merge - CODEOWNERS requiring specific human sign-off

  • Enforcement: OS permissions + server-side rules. Cannot be bypassed by the agent process.
  • What it catches: PR merges (server-side blocked), filesystem escape (permission denied), credential sharing (separate identity).
  • What it misses: Reading other branches via git object store (the .git directory must be readable for worktrees to function).

docs.title: Level 3: Full Repository Isolation
docs.text:
Each agent gets a separate clone (not worktree) with only the branches it needs. Or: agents work in containers with restricted git configurations.

  • Enforcement: The data simply isn't present.
  • What it catches: Everything — agents can't read what doesn't exist.
  • Cost: Significantly more disk, setup complexity, and fundamentally changes the worktree-based coordination model.

docs.title: Choosing the Right Level
docs.text:
The right isolation level depends on what you're optimizing for:

Priority Recommended Level Rationale Move fast, observe behavior 0 See what agents do, learn failure modes Prevent dangerous actions 1–2 Block merges and filesystem escape Enforce coordination model 2 Agents can see but not act beyond scope Prevent information leakage 3 Agents can't even read unauthorized content

For most multi-agent orchestration scenarios, Level 2 is the sweet spot. Agents can still observe other branches (which is often useful for understanding context), but they cannot act on them in ways that change shared state. The key insight is:

Reading is often acceptable. Writing — to the filesystem, to git branches, to GitHub PRs — is where coordination boundaries must be enforced.

This maps naturally to the principle of least privilege: agents get read access to the repository (necessary for their work) but write access is scoped to their task branch, and merge authority is reserved for humans (or the orchestrator acting on human-approved rules).

docs.title: Tunable Isolation
docs.text:
A key insight: the right isolation level is not a global constant. It varies along multiple axes:

  • By project: A personal side project may be fine at Level 0. A production codebase with CI/CD pipelines needs Level 2+.
  • By workstream: A workstream doing exploratory prototyping can be looser than one touching authentication code.
  • By task: Within a single graph, one task might need broad filesystem access (e.g., a "merger" agent resolving conflicts across the whole repo) while another should be tightly scoped (e.g., an implementer that only touches two specific files).

This means isolation should be a configurable, per-scope setting with inheritance and override:

Graph default:    Level 2 (OS identity separation)
  Workstream X:   (inherits Level 2)
    Task A:       (inherits Level 2)
    Task B:       Level 1 (needs broader access for cross-repo work)
  Workstream Y:   Level 0 (exploratory, want to observe behavior)

docs.title: Enforcement mechanisms map to tunability
docs.text:
The mechanisms available at each level lend themselves naturally to per-scope configuration:

Linux OS permissions are inherently tunable. A separate claude-agent user is the baseline, but ACLs (Access Control Lists) allow fine-grained per-directory, per-file read/write control. A task that needs broad access gets wider ACLs; a locked-down task gets restrictive ones. The orchestrator can set up ACLs as part of the task preparation step, scoped to the specific worktree and files the task should access.

GitHub fine-grained PATs are also tunable by design. Different tasks could use different tokens with different permission sets — or a single token with the minimum permissions any task needs, combined with branch protection rules that vary by branch pattern. A "merger" task might get a token with PR merge permissions; a standard implementer gets one without.

Git hooks can read environment variables set per-task by the orchestrator. The hook logic can vary based on which task is running: allow merges for a task that's supposed to merge, block them for everyone else.

Bubblewrap bind mounts are configured per-launch. The orchestrator can customize which directories are visible and writable for each tmux session, tailoring the filesystem sandbox to the task's declared paths.

docs.title: Configuration in the graph YAML
docs.text:
This fits naturally into the existing graph YAML schema, where per-task and per-workstream overrides are already the norm (model, role, tools, environment). An isolation field could specify the level and any overrides:

# Graph-level default
isolation:
  level: 2
  allow_merge: false

workstreams:
  - id: ws_explore
    isolation:
      level: 0        # override: observe behavior

tasks:
  - id: merge_conflicts
    isolation:
      level: 1
      allow_merge: true   # this task specifically needs merge authority

The exact schema is TBD — the right fields depend on what we learn during the observation period. But the principle is: declare the isolation contract alongside the task definition, so it's auditable and version-controlled just like everything else in the graph.

docs.title: Isolation as role contract
docs.text: Tunability reframes isolation from a pure security mechanism to part of the task's role contract. A "merger" agent's job is literally to cross boundaries that other agents can't — merging branches, resolving conflicts across the repo. Its isolation config declares what it's allowed to do, not just how locked-down it is. The isolation level becomes a capability declaration: "this agent has merge authority" is as much a part of its specification as "this agent is an implementer."

docs.title: Practical token and ACL management
docs.text:
Managing a unique GitHub PAT per task would be impractical. A more realistic approach is a small set of token tiers:

Tier Permissions Example use Read-only Contents: read, PRs: read Reviewer, auditor Standard Contents: read/write, PRs: read/write Implementer, test writer Elevated Standard + merge authority Merger, integration agent

Tasks declare which tier they need. The orchestrator selects the appropriate token at launch time. The tokens themselves are created once and reused across runs.

Similarly, filesystem access can be managed through ACL profiles rather than per-task ACL rules:

Profile Access Narrow Read/write only to declared paths.src and paths.test Standard Full worktree read/write Broad Worktree + read access beyond (e.g., for cross-repo tasks)

These profiles are applied by the orchestrator during task preparation (via setfacl or bubblewrap bind mounts), using the task's declared paths and isolation level to select the right profile.

docs.title: The "Ideal Agent" Behavior
docs.text:
What we actually want from an agent that encounters a dependency gap:

  1. Recognize the situation: "I need code that exists on another branch but hasn't been merged into my integration branch."

  2. Do not self-remedy: Don't merge, cherry-pick, or copy the code.

  3. Report clearly: Record the observation as a concern — "Task X's PR (#123) contains code I need. If merged, I could proceed."

  4. Fail gracefully: Mark the task as failed with a clear, actionable reason that tells the human operator exactly what to do.

This behavior requires both instruction (telling the agent what to do when blocked) and enforcement (preventing the agent from taking the shortcut even if it wants to). Neither alone is sufficient:

  • Instructions without enforcement: agent may override ("I know I'm not supposed to, but this will solve the problem...")
  • Enforcement without instructions: agent fails with a confusing error instead of a helpful diagnosis

docs.title: Isolation and Dependency Ordering: Two Complementary Defenses
docs.text:
The motivating example reveals two problems that interact:

  • Workstream dependency ordering prevents the situation from arising in the expected case. If the orchestrator waits for Workstream X's integration PR to merge before dispatching Task B, Agent B never encounters the gap.

  • Agent isolation ensures that when any unexpected gap occurs — including ones the orchestrator doesn't anticipate — agents cannot change shared state. They fail, report, and the human decides what to do.

Neither alone is sufficient:

Defense What it prevents What it doesn't prevent Workstream ordering only The specific cross-workstream gap Any other unexpected situation where an agent finds a "helpful" shortcut Agent isolation only Agents acting beyond their scope Agents being dispatched into situations where they can't succeed Both The gap doesn't arise, AND if something unexpected happens, agents can't self-remedy —

docs.title: Which to implement first?
docs.text:
There are two reasonable arguments:

Isolation first: Isolation is foundational. It affects every agent interaction, not just cross-workstream dependencies. It's also a safety net — with isolation in place, missing orchestrator features surface as clean failures instead of silent workarounds. You discover what's broken because agents tell you (by failing) rather than hiding it (by working around it). And isolation constraints are hard to retrofit — it's easier to build on a foundation of least-privilege than to tighten permissions after agents have learned to operate without them.

Workstream ordering first: Workstream dependency ordering is architecturally fundamental — it completes the orchestrator's scheduling model. The cross-workstream gap is a known, specific bug with a known fix. With proper ordering, the most dramatic isolation failure (the motivating example) simply cannot occur, because agents aren't dispatched into impossible situations. And the implementation is entirely within the orchestrator — no external setup (Linux users, PATs, branch protection) required.

docs.title: The chosen approach: ordering first, then deliberate observation
docs.text:
We chose to implement workstream dependency ordering first:

  • It's a contained code change within the orchestrator (no external setup)
  • It eliminates the most visible class of agent overreach
  • It completes the orchestrator's scheduling model

For isolation, we chose to defer enforcement and observe deliberately. Rather than immediately locking down agent permissions, we continue running at Level 0 (trust-based) while actively monitoring for "overly resourceful" agent behavior during e2e testing.

The rationale: the observation period has value in itself. With only one dramatic incident to learn from, we don't yet have a clear picture of where to draw boundary lines. Every agent workaround we observe before implementing isolation is a data point that informs the eventual design:

  • Which actions are genuinely dangerous? (merge a PR to main)
  • Which are harmless or even beneficial? (read another branch for context)
  • Which are in a gray area that depends on the situation?

If we lock down isolation immediately, these behaviors become permission errors instead of observations. We learn what we blocked, but not what we might have wanted to allow. A premature isolation design risks over-constraining (agents fail on things that would have been fine) or under-constraining (we miss a category of overreach we hadn't imagined).

With workstream ordering in place, the most likely trigger for "creative" agent behavior — being dispatched into an impossible situation — goes away. The remaining surface area for overreach is smaller, less predictable, and exactly the kind of thing worth observing before designing restrictions.

docs.title: What to watch for during the observation period
docs.text:
When running e2e tests, look for agents that:

  • Access other branches' content — git show, git log, cherry-pick from branches outside their task scope. Is this helpful context-gathering or boundary violation?
  • Modify shared state — merge PRs, push to branches they don't own, create issues or comments. Any write to shared state is a candidate for future enforcement.
  • Navigate outside their worktree — cd to the main repo, read files from other worktrees, access the human operator's home directory.
  • Use credentials beyond their scope — gh operations on other repos, API calls that aren't part of the task workflow.
  • Work around missing dependencies silently — the most insidious pattern. The agent finds a way to proceed but doesn't report the workaround. Look for unexplained success in tasks that should have been blocked.

Each observation informs whether the eventual isolation design should block, allow, or require-and-report that category of action.

docs.title: Implementation Notes (agentrelay-specific)
docs.text: This section tracks implementation decisions as they evolve.

docs.title: Current state (as of 2026-03-25)
docs.text:
• Agents run as the human user, sharing SSH keys and gh credentials
  • Worktrees provide directory-level separation but share .git
  • No filesystem isolation (agents can cd anywhere)
  • No git hooks restricting agent operations
  • Agent instructions say nothing about isolation boundaries
  • Task-to-task dependency ordering works; workstream-to-workstream does not

docs.title: Next: Workstream dependency ordering
docs.text: Wire cross-workstream dependency checking into the orchestrator's dispatch logic. A task should not be dispatched until all upstream workstreams that contain its dependencies have their integration PRs merged to main (or the dependency's code is otherwise available in the task's worktree).

docs.title: Future: Level 2 isolation (after observation period)
docs.text:
• Separate claude-agent Linux user with restricted GitHub PAT
  • Fine-grained PAT: Contents (read/write) + Pull Requests (read/write) scoped to target repo only — but PR merge blocked by branch protection
  • GitHub branch protection: require approval from CODEOWNER (human)
  • Git hooks with IS_AI_AGENT=true env var to block local merges
  • Orchestrator sets env var when launching agent tmux sessions
  • Agent instructions updated: explicit guidance on what to do when blocked by a missing dependency
  • Specific restrictions informed by observations collected during the observation period

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
• Should agents be able to read other branches' content, or should we pursue Level 3 isolation to prevent even that? (Collect observations before deciding.)
  • How should the orchestrator detect and surface the "dependency not yet merged" situation proactively (before the agent discovers it)?
  • What's the right failure mode: mark task as failed (requiring human intervention to retry), or have the orchestrator automatically wait and retry when the dependency is merged?
  • Where is the right boundary between "resourceful problem-solving we want to encourage" and "coordination violation we need to prevent"? The answer likely depends on the specific action and context — a one-size-fits-all rule may not exist.

This document is a living draft. It will be updated as the isolation strategy is implemented and tested with real multi-agent graph runs.

docs.title: OPENROUTER BIFROST RUST
docs.text:
You said Scanning more through the OpenRouter docs, it looks like it provides an interface for accessing a huge variety of different models from different providers. Is that correct?

Gemini said You are absolutely correct. OpenRouter is an aggregator (or unified gateway) for the entire AI ecosystem.

Instead of you having to sign up for a dozen different accounts (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, Meta, etc.), you sign up for OpenRouter once, and they handle the "plumbing" to connect you to all of them.

How it works in practice Think of it like a "Universal Remote" or a "Travel Booking Site" for AI:

One API Key: You only need one key to talk to 500+ models.

One Billing Account: You add credits to OpenRouter, and those credits are spent regardless of whether you’re using a high-end model like GPT-5.4 or a free model like Llama 4.

Standardized Language: It "translates" everything into the OpenAI Chat Completions format. This is why your Claude Code session can talk to a Google Gemini model without needing a specific "Gemini plugin"—it just treats everything like a standard OpenAI call.

What kind of "Variety" are we talking about? As of early 2026, the variety is staggering. OpenRouter categorizes them so you don't get overwhelmed:

Category Examples (Current 2026 Models) Why use them? Frontier Models GPT-5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro Best-in-class reasoning, coding, and complex agency. Open-Weight Models Llama 4 (Meta), Qwen 3 (Alibaba), Mistral Large 3 Often cheaper or more uncensored than frontier models. Specialized Models DeepSeek V3.2 (Coding), Olmo 3.1 (Research) Tuned for specific tasks like math or low-level systems programming. Free Models MiMo-V2, Nemotron 3 Nano Zero-cost models for simple tasks (summarization, basic chat). The "Providers" vs. "Models" distinction It's helpful to understand that OpenRouter doesn't usually "run" these models themselves. They connect you to Inference Providers.

Example: If you want to use Llama 3.3, OpenRouter might give you a choice of 5 different providers (like Groq, Together, DeepInfra, or Lambda) that are all running that same model.

Auto-Routing: By default, OpenRouter will automatically pick the one that is currently the cheapest or has the lowest latency at that exact second.

It accepts the exact same commands and data structures that Claude Code sends, but then translates them to work with models like GPT-5, Gemini, or Llama 4.

How to set it up To make your local Claude Code session use a different model via OpenRouter, you just need to set three environment variables in your terminal before launching claude.

Bash

docs.title: 1. Point the base URL to OpenRouter's Anthropic-compatible endpoint

Uncensored Coding: If Anthropic's safety filters are blocking you from working on a specific security-related project, you can swap to a more permissive model.

Redundancy: If Anthropic's API goes down, you can switch your "brain" to Google Gemini in seconds without changing your workflow.

Specialization: Sometimes a specific model (like DeepSeek-Coder) is simply better at a niche language or framework than Claude is.

While OpenRouter tries to translate this perfectly, you might occasionally see "hallucinated" tool calls or weird formatting if you use a much weaker model. For the best experience, stick to "Frontier" class models (GPT-4o/5, Gemini 1.5/2 Pro) when using them inside Claude Code.

You said Thanks. If I'm using an model in ClaudeCode via OpenRouter, is the context window limiit determined entirely by the model I've chosen?

Gemini said In 2026, the answer is no, it is not determined entirely by the model. It is a "two-key" system between the model's physical limit and the Claude Code client's internal "governor."

While OpenRouter will technically allow you to send as many tokens as the model supports (e.g., 2 million for Gemini 1.5 Pro or 1 million for GPT-5.4), Claude Code has its own internal logic for managing context that you have to keep in sync.

  1. The "Governor": Claude Code Auto-Compaction Claude Code is designed to be an autonomous agent. To prevent the context from getting "muddy" or too expensive, it uses a feature called Auto-Compaction.

Default Behavior: If Claude Code doesn't recognize the model name (which happens often with OpenRouter strings), it typically defaults to a 200,000 token safety limit.

The Problem: If you are using a model with a 1M context window but Claude Code thinks it only has 200k, the tool will start summarizing your conversation history and "forgetting" details once you hit ~190,000 tokens (95% capacity), even though the model could have handled much more.

  1. How to "Sync" the Context Window To ensure Claude Code uses the full power of your chosen OpenRouter model, you should use these two specific settings:

The [1m] Suffix When choosing your model in Claude Code (either via /model or the ANTHROPIC_MODEL env var), append [1m] to the ID.

Example: openai/gpt-5.4[1m]

This tells Claude Code: "Treat this as a high-capacity model and don't start compacting until we get close to 1 million tokens."

The Manual Override If you are using a model with a massive window (like Gemini's 2M), you should set the following environment variable before launching your session:

Bash export CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_COMPACT_WINDOW=2000000 This forces the Claude Code client to move its "compaction trigger" to 2 million tokens.

  1. Practical Limits in 2026 Even if you use a 1M+ context model, there are three reasons you might still want to keep the "governor" set lower:

"Context Rot": Most developers in 2026 agree that even with 1M tokens, model reasoning quality drops significantly (often called "the cliff") after about 300k–400k tokens.

Cost: A single request with 1 million tokens of context can cost anywhere from $3.00 to $15.00 depending on the model. Claude Code makes many requests in a loop; a single "fix this bug" task could easily burn $50 in five minutes if the context is full.

Latency: Sending 1 million tokens to the server and back takes significantly longer. Your "snappy" terminal session will start to feel sluggish.

Pro-Tip for Claude Code Users You can check your real-time context usage at any time during a session by typing:

/context

This will show you a breakdown of what is taking up space (files, tool outputs, or conversation history) and what the current cap is set to. If that cap doesn't match your model's capacity, you need to adjust your CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_COMPACT_WINDOW.

The CPU anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet (via OpenRouter) The Harness (Claude Code) The "Operating System" that provides tools, manages the filesystem, and handles context. Because Claude Code is arguably the best "coding harness" ever built, it can make even mid-tier models (like Llama 4 or GPT-5 Mini) feel like geniuses because it provides them with such high-quality context and tools.

  1. Setting up Fallbacks (Local <-> Cloud) This is a very common 2026 workflow for saving money. In a tool like OpenClaw (the leading open-source orchestrator right now), your config would look like this:

JSON { "routing": { "strategy": "fallback", "priority": [ "ollama/llama-4-8b", // 1st: Local (Free, fast, private) "openrouter/google/gemini-flash", // 2nd: Cheap Cloud (if local is down) "openrouter/anthropic/claude-opus" // 3rd: Heavyweight (if others fail) ] } } 3. For "Brain" work: (Refactoring architecture, debugging complex race conditions) The agent automatically scales up to a Frontier model via OpenRouter.

Production vs. Development: * Production apps use OpenRouter for its Provider Fallbacks (if Anthropic's server is down, OpenRouter instantly routes the same request to a different provider hosting the same model).

Developers use it for Model Fallbacks (switching from a local "small" brain to a cloud "big" brain).

Route A (Direct): If the task needs "Opus-level" reasoning, send it to your Anthropic Max account using your direct API key.

Route B (Prepaid): If the task is a simple refactor, send it to OpenRouter to use a cheap model like Llama 4.

Bifrost (The "Performance" Choice) Bifrost is written in Go and is currently the "gold standard" for agentic workflows.

Latency: It adds only 11 microseconds of overhead. In a long tmux-based task graph, this prevents "latency crawl."

LiteLLM (The "Community" Choice) LiteLLM is written in Python and is the most widely supported "universal remote."

Ease of Use: If your Orchestrator is written in Python, pip install litellm is the fastest way to get a routing layer working.

Deep Integrations: It has the most "pre-built" integrations for tools like LangChain, DSPy, and almost every 2026 agent framework.

Gemini said In 2026, the "Python vs. Rust" debate for AI orchestration has moved past simple performance benchmarks and into a discussion about Systemic Reliability.

Your dilemma is the classic one: Velocity (Python) vs. Industrial Integrity (Rust). Here is how to break down that decision for a TaskGraph Orchestrator in the current landscape.

  1. The Bottleneck Reality You are correct that Network I/O (waiting for Claude/OpenRouter) is currently the primary bottleneck. However, as your TaskGraph grows in complexity (e.g., 50+ dependent nodes running in parallel), a new bottleneck emerges: Orchestration Overhead.

Python's Tax: In 2026, benchmarks show that high-level Python agent frameworks (like LangGraph or CrewAI) can add 50–150ms of "framework latency" per turn just for state management, JSON validation, and async task switching.

Rust's Efficiency: Rust-based orchestrators (like the Rig framework or AutoAgents) have near-zero initialization time and handle state transitions in micro-seconds.

The 2026 Verdict: If your graph is "sparse" (3–5 tasks), Python is fine. If your graph is "dense" (massively parallel, self-correcting loops), the Python overhead starts to feel like "lag" in a video game—every action has a slight, perceptible delay.

  1. Why Rust Wins for Task Graphs If you decide to migrate, Rust’s core strengths align perfectly with the "Unit of Work" model you’ve built:

Fearless Concurrency: In Python, asyncio is great until you hit a CPU-bound task (like heavy local data processing or complex graph sorting) that blocks the event loop. Rust’s Tokio runtime and thread-safety mean you can launch 1,000 agents in parallel with zero risk of the "Global Interpreter Lock" (GIL) slowing down your management logic.

The "Ownership" Benefit: AI agents are notoriously messy with state. They often try to write to the same file or modify the same Task node simultaneously. Rust’s borrow checker forces you to solve these race conditions at compile-time. You won't get a "Task State Corrupted" error at 3 AM; the code simply won't compile until the logic is safe.

Resource Efficiency: As you noted, resource scarcity is a looming threat. A Rust orchestrator can run on a machine with 1/5th the RAM of a Python one. This matters if you eventually want to run your orchestrator on "Edge" hardware or a small VPS alongside your local models.

  1. The "Velocity" Counter-Argument Python’s ecosystem in 2026 is still the "Gravity Well" of AI.

Library Support: Every new MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool, every new OpenRouter feature, and every experimental Anthropic SDK will launch in Python first.

Prototyping: Changing your YAML schema or adding a new "Node Type" takes 5 minutes in Python. In Rust, you’ll be spending that time defining Structs, Enums, and Traits.

  1. My Recommendation: The "2026 Hybrid" Pattern Most advanced orchestrator projects in 2026 are moving toward a "Rust Core, Python Shell" model (similar to how Pydantic v2 and Polars are built).

Don't rewrite the whole thing yet. Instead, consider this roadmap:

Stay in Python for Velocity: Continue using Python to figure out the "Human-Agent Interaction" and the "Task Graph Logic." This is where the most frequent changes will happen.

Offload the "Engine" to Bifrost: Since you've already chosen Bifrost (which is written in Go), you are already getting the "Performance" benefits of a compiled language for the network and routing layer. Bifrost will handle the "scarcity" issues you mentioned (caching, compression, routing).

The "Rust Pivot" Point: Migrate to Rust ONLY when you hit one of these three walls:

State Complexity: You find yourself spending 40% of your time debugging "Heisenbugs" related to task dependencies and shared memory.

Scale: You want to run your orchestrator as a multi-tenant service for other people (where memory-per-user becomes your biggest cloud cost).

Safety: You start giving agents the ability to perform "High-Stakes" actions (like moving real money or deleting production databases), where Rust's type-safety becomes a legal/security requirement.

Summary If you are still "learning the landscape," stay in Python. The ability to pivot your design in an afternoon is more valuable than saving 100ms of CPU time.

Here is why your gut feeling about the switch is perfectly timed for where the ecosystem is heading this year.

  1. The "Bifrost" Precedent It’s worth noting that Bifrost (the gateway we discussed) followed this exact path. It started as a high-performance Go project, but the 2026 "Bifrost: Redux" version was rewritten in Rust.

The Reason: They found that as agentic tool-calling became more complex, the "nondeterministic" nature of other languages led to "mystery bugs" in tool state.

The Result: By switching to Rust, they achieved nanosecond-resolution backtesting and a type-system that makes it physically impossible for an agent to "forget" its tool context.

  1. Rust as a "Thoughtfulness Partner" You mentioned that Rust's ownership model helps keep things clean. In an orchestrator, this is a superpower.

In Python: You might pass a Task object to an agent, and while that agent is working, another part of your code accidentally modifies the task's state. You now have a "race condition" that is a nightmare to debug.

In Rust: The compiler will literally yell at you. It forces you to decide exactly who "owns" that task and who is allowed to look at it. You are forced to be thoughtful at the moment of creation, rather than the moment of failure.

  1. The 2026 "Harness" Stack If you do pivot to Rust, you won't be starting from scratch. Several "Thoughtful Engineering" frameworks have matured this year:

Rig (Rust): A high-level library for building portable, modular AI agents. It handles the "pluggable" part of your triad (Harness/Engine/Provider) natively.

AutoAgents (Rust Runtime): Specifically designed for the "Headless" mode you’re building. It excels at running hundreds of parallel units of work with near-zero memory overhead.

Strategy: "Code in Python, Think in Rust" Since you’re staying in Python for now, you can "prep" for the migration by adopting "Industrial Python" patterns that map 1:1 to Rust:

Strict Pydantic Models: Treat your YAML task definitions as rigid schemas. If it doesn't fit the model, fail fast.

Stateless Tasks: Ensure your task nodes don't rely on global variables. If a node needs data, it must be passed in explicitly (this mimics Rust’s "Borrowing").

Traceable Ownership: Keep a clear "Registry" of which agent is working on which tmux session.

Summary The gold rush for "velocity" is creating a lot of fragile software. By the time you switch to Rust, you’ll have a battle-tested design, and the resulting orchestrator will likely outlast the dozens of "flash-in-the-pan" Python frameworks that are populating GitHub today.

Would you like me to help you design a "State Machine" in Python using Pydantic? This would give you a very clear, Rust-like structure for how your task nodes transition from Pending → Running → Committed, making the eventual migration a breeze.

You said Thanks. Full disclosure - although I am a huge fan of the Rust language design principles, my Rust experience is very minimal. I did one small Rust project as an undergrad, and I've started reading through the "Rust book" many times, but always get pulled away from it by work on a project in Python or C++. It may be a bit challenging, but driving force behind migrating my Orchestrator to Rust is what I need to to really dive into Rust.

Gemini said This is a classic "Slay the Dragon" moment in a developer's journey. Using a complex, multi-threaded project like an Orchestrator as your "forcing function" is the most effective way to learn Rust, because it forces you to face the three "Final Bosses" of the language simultaneously: Ownership, Async, and Trait-based Abstraction.

Given your background in C++ and Python, you have a unique advantage—and a unique set of "unlearning" to do.

  1. The C++/Python to Rust Mental Shift Since you’ve worked with C++, you already understand pointers and memory. But Rust will feel like "C++ with a very pedantic manager."

From C++ (RAII) to Rust (Ownership): In C++, you hope your RAII handles everything, but you can still accidentally use-after-free. In Rust, the compiler tracks the "alive-ness" of your Task nodes. If you pass a Task to an Agent thread, the Orchestrator "loses" it unless you explicitly use an Arc (Atomic Reference Count).

From Python (Asyncio) to Rust (Tokio): Python's asyncio is a single-threaded loop. Rust’s tokio (the 2026 industry standard) is a multi-threaded work-stealing scheduler. You will run into the Send + Sync traits—Rust's way of ensuring that if an Agent is working on a task, it’s physically impossible for another thread to mutate that task's state at the same time.

  1. Your 2026 Rust "Starter Kit" Since you are building a Task Graph Orchestrator, don't start from a blank main.rs. Use these 2026-era building blocks to maintain velocity while you learn:

Component The "Best in Class" 2026 Crate Why for your project? The Engine (LLM) rig-core The most ergonomic Rust library for LLMs. It handles the "OpenRouter" and "Anthropic" switching natively with one unified Trait. The Graph (Nodes) petgraph The gold standard for DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs). It will handle your dependency sorting and cycle detection. The Parallelism tokio You need this for your async task runner. It’s the closest thing to Python's asyncio but significantly more powerful. The CLI/Harness autoagents-llm A modular framework that specifically handles "Agent Execution" and "Memory Layers." You can use it as a reference for your "Harness" layer. 3. A Recommended "Migration Roadmap" Instead of a total rewrite, build the "Rust Core" first and keep your Python orchestrator as the "manager."

Phase 1: The "Engine" Proxy (CLI tool) Rewrite just the part that talks to OpenRouter/Anthropic in Rust using rig-core.

Goal: Learn how Rust handles JSON, API calls, and environment variables.

Success: You can call my-rust-cli --model gpt-5 from your Python script.

Phase 2: The "Graph Runner" (The Brain) Move the logic that decides which task to run next into Rust using petgraph.

Goal: Learn about Ownership. You’ll have to decide how the "Graph" owns the "Tasks" and how it hands them off to workers.

Success: Python sends a YAML file to Rust; Rust returns a sorted execution plan.

Phase 3: The "Full Harness" (The Agent) Move the tmux/process-spawning logic into Rust.

Goal: Learn Async and PTYs. Managing terminal sessions (tmux) from Rust involves a lot of asynchronous I/O and process signaling.

Success: The entire tmux lifecycle is managed by a single, high-performance Rust binary.

  1. Why this is the "Thoughtful" Path You mentioned that "Dev thoughtfulness is scarce." Rust is the ultimate tool for a thoughtful developer because it forbids shortcuts.

In Python, it's easy to say "just make this a global dictionary" or "just ignore this potential race condition for now." Rust won't let you. It forces you to architect the State Machine of your Task Graph correctly on day one. By the time your Orchestrator is running in Rust, you won't just have a faster tool—you'll have a mathematically verified workflow.

The First Step for your Project Don't go back to the "Rust Book" chapter 1. Instead, go to the Rig-rs documentation. Try to write a 20-line Rust script that:

Connects to OpenRouter.

Sends a prompt.

Prints the response.

Once you have that "Hello World" through OpenRouter, you've already conquered the "Provider" part of your triad.

Would you like me to generate a "Skeleton" Rust file using rig-core and tokio that replicates your current "Task Node" structure? It might be the "bridge" you need to finally leave the Python nest.

You said Thanks. To me, it sounds like Rust is what C++ would be if we through away a lot of the old/primitive part of the language, and forced usage of modern things like smart pointers.

Gemini said That is a brilliant way to frame it. If C++ is a powerful, manual-shift sports car from the 1970s that you’ve spent decades maintaining, Rust is that same car re-engineered in 2026 with a modern aerospace-grade flight computer that refuses to let you crash.

For a C++ developer, the mental bridge to Rust is actually quite short once you realize that Rust didn't invent new concepts; it just took the best practices you already try to follow in C++ and made them compulsory.

  1. The "Forced Smart Pointer" Reality Your intuition about smart pointers is spot on. In Rust, you don't "use" smart pointers as an alternative to raw pointers; you use them because the language has effectively deleted the raw pointer from the "safe" side of the house.

C++ Concept Rust Equivalent The "Modernized" Difference std::unique_ptr Box In Rust, this is the default way to put something on the heap. You don't have to remember to use it; if you want heap allocation, you Box it. std::shared_ptr Arc Atomic Reference Counting. Rust splits this into Rc (single-threaded) and Arc (multi-threaded). The compiler won't let you use a non-atomic Rc across threads, preventing the race conditions that haunt C++ devs. std::weak_ptr Weak Used the same way to break circular references, but with a more robust API for checking if the data still exists. 2. The "Old/Primitive" Parts (What got thrown away) When you say Rust "threw away the primitives," what it really did was eliminate the "Ambiguous Reference."

In C++, a pointer T* is a mystery box. Does it own the memory? Is it null? Is it pointing to a single object or an array?

In Rust, a reference &T or &mut T is a contract. * The compiler (the "Borrow Checker") acts like a senior architect who reads every line of your code and says: "I see you gave this Agent a reference to the TaskGraph, but you didn't prove that the TaskGraph will live as long as the Agent. Fix it or I won't build."

  1. Why your C++ background is a "Cheat Code" Most people struggle with Rust because they don't understand how memory actually works (the Python/Java crowd). Because you know C++, you already have the "mental model" of the heap and the stack.

You aren't learning what memory is; you're just learning how to describe it to a very strict compiler.

The "Ownership" struggle: You’ll initially feel like you're fighting the compiler.

The "Aha!" moment: You'll realize the compiler is just catching the exact bugs you would have spent three days debugging in C++ with gdb or valgrind.

  1. A Small Correction on "Bifrost" Just a quick "peer-to-peer" correction: Earlier, I mentioned Bifrost as a performance leader. While it follows the same "thoughtful/efficient" philosophy as Rust, it’s actually written in Go. * The Lesson: In 2026, the industry is splitting. Go is winning for "high-speed networking services" (like gateways), while Rust is winning for "complex state-heavy systems" (like your Orchestrator).

Your choice of Rust for the TaskGraph is the correct one. A graph with moving parts and complex dependencies is exactly the kind of "State Machine" where Go's garbage collector or Python's async loop would eventually become a headache.

The Best Way to Start Since you’ve "started the book" many times, my advice is: Stop reading and start porting.

Take your simplest Python Task Node—just the part that defines a unit of work—and try to define it as a Rust Struct. 1. Define the struct. 2. Try to pass it to a function. 3. Watch the compiler complain about "Move" and "Borrow." 4. That is where the real learning happens.

Would you like me to show you a "Side-by-Side" of a C++ class vs. a Rust struct for a Task Node? It might help you see exactly which "old habits" Rust is asking you to trade in.

Your Kernel State chats aren’t used to improve our models. Gemini is AI and can make mistakes. Your privacy & Gemini

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
These YAML files are validated in tests so they stay in sync with the current TaskGraphBuilder schema.

  • minimal.yaml: minimal linear graph.
  • workstreams.yaml: parent/child workstreams with explicit mapping.
  • mixed-default-and-explicit-workstreams.yaml: explicit default plus additional workstream.

docs.title: Role: You are a SPEC_WRITER tasked with writing specifications for part of a software project. Follow the instructions below to complete the task.
Tools
docs.text: • pixi: Use pixi run to execute all Python commands, tests, and scripts. Do not use bare python, pytest, or pip — always prefix with pixi run.

docs.title: What to Do
docs.text:
Scope: write API stubs only. Define class, function, and method signatures with type hints and docstrings. Function and method bodies should be raise NotImplementedError. Do not write working logic.

  1. Create specification file(s) at: src/agentrelaydemos/bounded_queue.py In these files, create class signatures and docstrings that define the API contract for the items described in the Task Details section below. The implementation body of each method should be raise NotImplementedError.

As you work, record any concerns you encounter: - Design concerns (spec contradictions, ambiguities): agentrelay-concern --message "..." - Ops concerns (build errors, missing deps, tooling friction): agentrelay-ops-concern --message "..."

docs.title: Submitting Your Work
docs.text:
After completing the work above:

  1. Commit and push all changes to branch agentrelay/role-pipeline/spec_bounded_queue.
  2. Complete the task (creates PR and signals the orchestrator): 
    agentrelay-complete --title "short summary of changes" --body "## Summary

- what was done"
    Provide a meaningful PR title (concise) and body (markdown with a ## Summary section). Any recorded concerns are automatically appended to the PR body.

If you made no code changes (e.g., review-only work), complete without a PR: 

agentrelay-complete-no-pr

If you cannot complete the work, signal failure instead: 

agentrelay-failed --reason "reason for failure"

Important: The orchestrator is waiting for the signal. Do not skip step 2.

docs.title: Task Details
docs.text:
Create the specification for BoundedQueue[T] class — a generic, fixed-capacity FIFO queue.

Constructor: - init(capacity: int) — capacity must be a positive integer (raise ValueError if capacity <= 0).

Core operations: - push(item: T) -> None — Add item to the back of the queue. If the queue is at capacity, the oldest item is automatically evicted to make room for the new item.

  • pop() -> T — Remove and return the front item. Raise IndexError if the queue is empty.

  • peek() -> T — Return the front item without removing it. Raise IndexError if the queue is empty.

Query methods: - is_full() -> bool — True when the current length equals capacity. - len() -> int — Current number of items in the queue. - capacity (read-only property) -> int — The maximum number of items.

Error handling: All mutating methods must validate preconditions and raise appropriate exceptions. push() raises OverflowError when the queue has reached capacity. pop() and peek() raise IndexError when the queue is empty.

docs.title: Prototype v01 Docs
docs.text: This section documents the runnable v01 prototype in src/agentrelay/prototypes/v01/.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: These docs are maintained as a reference for the current executable workflow while src/agentrelay/ evolves into the long-term architecture.

docs.title: Documents
docs.text:
• Workflow Description
  • Operations Guide
  • Design Decisions
  • History
  • Backlog

docs.title: Backlog
docs.text: Capture ideas here immediately — don't interrupt the current task to implement them. One item per bullet. Add a brief note if the idea needs context to make sense later. Move items to docs/HISTORY.md when done (with the PR number).

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Retry-with-context loops for TDD groups — when TEST_REVIEWER returns a CONCERNS verdict, write a structured retry-context file to the signal directory and re-run TEST_WRITER with accumulated failure history. The agent sees all previous failure reasons without any special retry logic inside agents — just files. Cap with a max_retries YAML field to bound run time. This is the natural complement to machine-readable verdicts and converts a hard stop into a repair cycle. Implementation touches: orchestrator loop in run_graph.py, TDD prompt builders, and optionally AgentTask / YAML schema for the retry cap.

  • Human escalation checkpoints — add an optional escalation: human flag to tasks. When set, the orchestrator pauses after the review step (or after max_retries auto-escalation) and polls for a human-written human_approval.txt in the signal directory before proceeding. A lighter escalation: notify level could log a desktop-style message and continue automatically. Default is auto (current behavior). Implementation touches: YAML schema, AgentTask data model, orchestrator dispatch loop.

  • Pluggable agent harness interface — design and implement a clean abstraction layer between agentrelay's core orchestration code and the specific coding assistant frameworks/harnesses it uses to launch agents. The interface would decouple our orchestrator from Claude Code CLI specifics, making it straightforward to plug in support for GitHub Copilot, OpenAI Codex, Gemini Code Assist, or other coding assistants. Key aspects of the interface:

  • Abstract over agent launch mechanics (CLI invocation, API calls, protocol-specific dialog handling)
  • Standardize how agents receive task prompts and deliver results (currently assumes tmux + sentinel files)
  • Define per-harness capabilities (what models available, cost/latency profile, token limits, concurrency constraints)

  • Provide hooks for harness-specific setup/teardown (e.g. auth, dependency installation)

  • Multi-agent backend support — implement support for dispatching tasks to different coding assistants (e.g. OpenAI Codex CLI, GitHub Copilot agent, Gemini Code Assist CLI) using the pluggable harness interface described above. Concrete changes:

  • Add an AgentBackend enum or dataclass (e.g. CLAUDE, COPILOT, CODEX, GEMINI, …) with per-backend harness implementation
  • Add an optional backend field to AgentTask (defaults to CLAUDE for backwards compat); expose it in the graph YAML too
  • Implement harness adapters that satisfy the pluggable interface for each supported backend, handling backend-specific launch, prompt delivery, and result collection

  • Consider whether worktree_task_runner.py (the agent-side API for writing signal files) needs to be harness-agnostic, or if each harness simply calls the same sentinel-file convention

  • Reasoning-model task analysis and assistant routing — before dispatching any tasks, run a planning step in which a strong reasoning model (e.g. Claude Sonnet or Opus via the Anthropic API) inspects each AgentTask and produces structured estimates: rough complexity tier (trivial / moderate / complex), expected output size / context pressure, and a recommended backend. Inputs to the analysis: task description, AgentRole, position in the dependency graph (leaf vs. fan-in), and any available codebase context. The recommendation can then be written back to AgentTask.backend (once that field exists — see item above) before the orchestrator loop starts. Optional extension: pair the complexity estimate with a simple cost model (estimated tokens × per-token price for each candidate backend) so the orchestrator can weight task difficulty, model capability, and cost together when making routing decisions — e.g. route trivial tasks to a cheaper model and reserve Opus-class agents for complex fan-in tasks. Results could be logged to a routing_plan.json alongside run_info.json for auditability.

  • Agents Note Possible Improvements in Project Infrastructure — If agents encounter things that slow them down (e.g. errors for certain gh commands that have to be re-run with new/different API) they make note of these in a .md file somewhere. Possible locations: under docs/, or under .workflow/ (docs may be better due to persistence?). Info provided by agent can note the hiccup and may suggest ways to fix (e.g. add something to CLAUDE.md, install a new package, etc.)

docs.title: Improvements
docs.text:
• Live run_state.json — a mutable per-run document at .workflow/<graph>/run_state.json that tracks each task's status, attempt count, and timestamps in real time. Currently agentrelay reconstructs state from scattered signal files on restart (hydration); an explicit state document would make that logic simpler and more robust (missing signal file = wrong state today). Would also serve as the natural place to record retry attempt counts for the retry-with-context loop feature. Richer than the existing run_info.json (which is a one-time snapshot of start HEAD + timestamp, not a live state document).

  • Structured audit log — an append-only .workflow/<graph>/audit.jsonl where each line is a timestamped JSON event: task dispatched, signal received, verdict parsed, retry attempted, merge completed, escalation triggered, etc. Currently agentrelay writes agent.log (raw tmux scrollback) and summary.md (PR body), which are human-readable but not machine-queryable. An audit log would enable grep/jq analysis across runs, per-task duration statistics, and retry rate tracking. Most useful once retry loops and escalation are in place — those add many new event types worth logging.

  • GitHub Actions for pre-merge checks — consider running pixi run check (format + typecheck + tests) via a GitHub Actions workflow on every PR instead of relying on the PR checklist. Pros: automated, can't be forgotten, visible as a required status check. Cons: slower feedback loop, needs pixi/conda setup in CI, removes Claude Code's ability to catch and fix failures before they hit the PR. Worth deciding whether Claude Code running checks locally or CI running them remotely is a better fit for this project's workflow.

  • Automate Generation/Specification of Completion Gate — How? Can Spec Writer do it? I don't think so because completion gate typically involves running test files, and Spec Writer doesn't know test details. Maybe this means we really do (almost?) always need the .md file under specs/. Although it may not make sense for the Spec Writer to put test specs/requirements as comments in a src file, it does seem reasonable for the Spec Writer to put testing requirements in the .md file.

docs.title: Ideas / Maybe
docs.text: • Agent-generated graph specs — a pre-run planning step (Claude Opus/Sonnet via the Anthropic API, not an interactive session) reads a natural-language project description and produces a valid graph.yaml that drives agentrelay's normal execution. This would lower the barrier to adopting the system for large or complex projects where authoring the task graph is itself a design problem. Pairs naturally with the reasoning-model routing item under Features above (the routing analysis and spec generation could be a single planning step). Speculative: requires significant prompt engineering and validation that the agent reliably produces valid YAML.

docs.title: agentrelay — Design Decisions
docs.text: Decisions made during early design discussions, with rationale. Ordered roughly from highest-level to most specific.

docs.title: No orchestration frameworks
docs.text:
Decision: Build directly on the Anthropic API, subprocess, and asyncio. Do not use LangChain, LangGraph, CrewAI, or similar.

Rationale: Frameworks add abstraction that obscures what's actually happening and makes debugging harder. The operations here (subprocess calls, file I/O, async polling) are straightforward enough that the framework overhead isn't justified. Staying close to the metal keeps the system debuggable and easy to reason about.

docs.title: Tmux for agent visibility
docs.text:
Decision: Launch worktree agents as interactive claude sessions in tmux panes, not as headless claude -p calls.
